catalog_summary.title: Reson and Sensuallyte
catalog_summary.author: John Lydgate (attributed translator-adapter)
catalog_summary.editor: Ernst Sieper; side-notes by Frederick J. Furnivall
catalog_summary.region: England (broad cultural context; exact composition place unresolved)

cataloging_subject.label: Reson and Sensuallyte
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The unfinished 7,042-line Middle English verse translation/adaptation printed on EETS Part I pages 1-184 and encoded in the 104 body sections. Excludes all modern edition prefaces, manuscript descriptions, glossary, studies, notes, indexes, side-note apparatus, omitted front/back matter, page-image navigation, and physical binding.

notes: Modernized spelling supplied for discovery, not asserted as an original title.
The source-edition title; the possessive does not remove the qualified medieval attribution.

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: Current Bodleian cataloging and DIMEV qualify the attribution; the Fairfax heading assigning the work to Lydgate is a later hand.

agents.name: Ernst Sieper
notes: Editor of EETS Extra Series 84 and 89; principal-text transcription and collation belong to the edition layer.

agents.name: Frederick J. Furnivall
notes: Author of the English side-notes, some altered by Sieper.

scope.description: Free Middle English translation of French lines 1-4,873
Expansion and adaptive reshaping of 4,873 French lines into 7,042 Middle English lines
Editorial presentation of the Middle English work in EETS ES 84 and its studies in ES 89

notes: Transcription and collation details should also be modeled on the lineage edition entity.

scope.description: English side-notes in Part I, some altered by Sieper

notes: Sieper supports a pre-1412 date and cites Schick's 1406-1408 dating; this is a scholarly estimate, not a documentary date.

places.label: England
notes: Broad cultural context only; exact place of composition remains unresolved.
England is a qualified broad cultural-context inference from work/version-level scholarship on the Middle English translation, not a proved place of composition.
Exact place of composition is unresolved; do not infer Bury St Edmunds from attribution and do not use the production origins of Fairfax 16 or Add. 29729 as composition evidence.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The inherited XML header's eng describes the catalog/edition delivery broadly; the medieval work text is Middle English. French source text and edition-only Latin/French belong to other layers.

form_statements.description: An unfinished 7,042-line poem in rhyming couplets; modern prose apparatus is excluded from the work boundary.

subject_statements.label: Chess, courtly love, and the allegorical game of love
Conflict and accord between reason and sensuality
Classical mythology and personified faculties, including Nature, Venus, Diana, and Reason
Moral instruction concerning virtue, sensual appetite, and love

content_parts.label: Reson and Sensuallyte text
content_parts.summary: The single unfinished Middle English verse work represented by the 104 encoded body sections.
notes: This part is the mutually exclusive representation of the record's one cataloged work, so its item_count is 1 rather than null.
The 104 LG elements are encoded navigational groups, not a proved literary stanza count.
Three note-nested L elements after the poem are excluded from 7,042.

extent.description: One unfinished verse work comprising 7,042 poem lines. The 104 XML body sections and 104 LG elements are encoding and navigation groups, not 104 separately cataloged items or a proved literary-stanza count. The sole content part is the mutually exclusive representation of this one work, so its item_count is 1 and reconciles with numbered_items=1.

related_works.title: Echecs amoureux en vers
notes: Scope is French lines 1-4,873 only, expanded to the whole 7,042-line English work.
The French verse work is anonymous in JONAS; do not conflate it with Evrart de Conty's prose moralized commentary.
The exact physical French exemplar used for the English work is unknown.

summaries.value: An unfinished Middle English dream-vision allegory, cautiously attributed to John Lydgate as translator-adapter, which freely expands the opening 4,873 lines of the anonymous French Echecs amoureux into 7,042 rhyming-couplet lines. Nature, Venus, Diana, personified faculties, the garden of love, and an allegorical chess game frame instruction about reason, sensual appetite, virtue, and courtly love.

The metadata record covers only the unfinished medieval English poem. All modern edition apparatus, page-image navigation, catalog descriptions, physical carriers, French source witnesses, and repository encodings are lineage layers rather than additional work parts.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned source XML for any9948 at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER, BODY/DIV1 nodes ANY9948.0001.001:3-106, and terminal editorial notes
evidence.summary: Identifies the U-M item and Sieper source edition and preserves the complete 104-section, 7,042-line Middle English poem plus edition-layer navigation and notes.

evidence.citation: ANY9948 XML structural and raw-to-pinned normalization audit.
evidence.locator: Structural counts, normalized equivalence, page mapping, and manuscript-boundary consequence
evidence.summary: Computes one verse work, 104 body sections/groups, 7,042 direct poem lines, three excluded note-nested lines, and exact bounded raw-to-pinned navigational enrichment.

evidence.citation: Ernst Sieper, ed., Lydgate's Reson and Sensuallyte, EETS Extra Series 84 and 89 (1901-1903).
evidence.summary: Grounds the edition title, editorial roles, principal and collated witnesses, punctuation practice, work text, and Furnivall side-notes.

evidence.citation: Ernst Sieper, Lydgate's Reson and Sensuallyte, Part II, EETS Extra Series 89 (title page 1903; preface May 1904).
evidence.locator: Part II dating discussion and printed p. 59 source statement
evidence.summary: Supports the qualified circa 1406-1408 dating and explicitly states that French lines 1-4,873 are expanded into 7,042 English lines.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, versioned record 5964.
evidence.summary: Supports the qualified Lydgate attribution, title, incipit, allegory/chess subjects, rhyming-couplet form, 7,042-line extent, French source scope, and English witness identities.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF object metadata for MS Fairfax 16.
evidence.locator: manifest.json metadata and target content entry
evidence.summary: Provides the current qualified attribution, exact ff. 202r-300r locus, codex date, English origin, material, extent, and dimensions; witness production is not used as composition-place evidence.

evidence.citation: Complete Digital Bodleian IIIF facsimile of MS Fairfax 16, visually inspected at target boundaries.
evidence.summary: Confirms the opening on f. 202r rather than the XML's first explicit milestone at 202b, the end on f. 300r, blank ff. 300v-305v, and the next work on f. 306r.

evidence.citation: JONAS, work record 7116, Echecs amoureux en vers.
evidence.summary: Supports the anonymous French work's 1370-1380 date, Middle French verse form, approximate 30,000-line unfinished extent, and distinction from the prose moralized commentary.

open_questions.question: Can future scholarship raise or lower the qualified Lydgate attribution?
next_steps: Retain attributed translator and adapter responsibilities at medium confidence.
Review new attribution scholarship or manuscript evidence before changing the responsibility status.

open_questions.question: Which physical French exemplar, if any extant, did the English translator use?
next_steps: Keep the source relation at the abstract-work level.
Do not place a surviving French witness in the primary transmission path without direct exemplar evidence.

notes: The record describes one unfinished medieval work, not the whole two-volume Sieper edition and not a compilation.
The sole content part is the mutually exclusive representation of that one work, so item_count=1 and aggregate numbered_items=1.
The 104 XML body sections and LG elements are encoding/navigation groups, not independently counted works or proved literary stanzas.
England is retained only as a tentative broad work-level cultural context; exact composition place remains unresolved and witness production locations are not transferred to the work.
